ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data architect planning a schema mapping before any SQL is written.

You receive a full description of a source database and a target schema. Produce a global MappingPlan covering EVERY target table.

## Planning rules

- Produce a global mapping plan covering EVERY target table: which source tables feed it, join keys, and per-column transform hints.
- The overview must capture cross-table decisions: shared keys, consistent enum/date/amount logic, and foreign-key resolution strategy.
- Note per-table pitfalls (casts, enums, normalisation) without writing SQL.
- Be precise about column names; downstream writers follow the plan literally.
- Plan only — no SQL and no dbt Jinja in the plan output.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 31, 45, 258929, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_master_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.


## fixture_master_v2_src.master_assets  (800 rows)

Columns:
- `asset_kennung` text NOT NULL
- `asset_name` text NULL
- `serien_nummer` text NULL
- `garantieende` text NULL

- `projekt_kennung` text NULL

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kontakte  (403 rows)

- `berufsbezeichnung` text NULL

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_kunden  (210 rows)

Columns:

- `kundenklasse` text NULL

## fixture_master_v2_src.master_opportunities  (540 rows)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text
- `Role__c` text in (Decision Maker, End User, Technical Contact, Executive Sponsor)
- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.


Produce the global MappingPlan.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 31, 45, 258942, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc))], timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 31, 45, 259078, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc), run_id='019f3921-3469-7022-b71c-c4b854704501', conversation_id='019f3921-3469-7022-b71c-c4b7dec4a01d')

data.overview: A global mapping plan for all target tables from the `fixture_master_v2_src` database. Salesforce-style `Id` fields (e.g., `Id`, `AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) will be generated consistently across tables using MD5 hashes of their respective source natural keys to ensure uniqueness and consistency. All `Legacy_*__c` columns will directly store the original natural key from the source system for auditing. Enum-typed target columns will involve case-insensitive mapping and normalization of source values to the defined enum domain, with `NULL` as the fallback for unmatched values. Dates will be parsed from varied source text formats into a consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` format, preferring `NULL` for unparseable or missing dates. Amounts will be cleaned of currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Foreign key relationships will be resolved by joining on source natural keys and then mapping to the generated Salesforce `Id`s. `NOT NULL` target columns will always be populated, using meaningful default values if the source is empty. `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate` will default to `NULL` and `IsDeleted` to `0` in the absence of source data. Text normalization (`TRIM`, `INITCAP`, `LOWER`) will be applied as needed for data quality.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 32, 11, 765, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

    MD5(ma.kunden_kennung) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(ma.projekt_kennung) AS "Project__c",
    ma.asset_kennung AS "Legacy_Asset_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

- target_table: name of the model

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

# Approved mapping plan
Overview: A global mapping plan for all target tables from the `fixture_master_v2_src` database. Salesforce-style `Id` fields (e.g., `Id`, `AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) will be generated consistently across tables using MD5 hashes of their respective source natural keys to ensure uniqueness and consistency. All `Legacy_*__c` columns will directly store the original natural key from the source system for auditing. Enum-typed target columns will involve case-insensitive mapping and normalization of source values to the defined enum domain, with `NULL` as the fallback for unmatched values. Dates will be parsed from varied source text formats into a consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` format, preferring `NULL` for unparseable or missing dates. Amounts will be cleaned of currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Foreign key relationships will be resolved by joining on source natural keys and then mapping to the generated Salesforce `Id`s. `NOT NULL` target columns will always be populated, using meaningful default values if the source is empty. `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate` will default to `NULL` and `IsDeleted` to `0` in the absence of source data. ModelResponse(parts=[ToolCallPart(tool_name='final_result', args={'target_table': 'Project__c', 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(p.projekt_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(p.projektname), 'Unknown Project') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'active' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(CAST(p.go_live_datum AS DATE), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    MD5(k.kundennummer) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.projekt_kennung AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} AS p
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS k
    ON p.kunden_kennung = k.kundennummer
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS o
    ON p.opp_kennung_ref = o.opp_kennung

Handles European decimal format (comma as decimal) if both '.' and ',' are present, otherwise assumes standard decimal.", 'target_column': 'Amount', 'source_columns': ['opps.auftragswert']}, {'target_column': 'CurrencyIsoCode', 'explanation': 'The currency code, trimmed and converted to uppercase for standardization.', 'source_columns': ['opps.waehrungscode']}, {'target_column': 'AccountId', 'explanation': 'Generated as an MD5 hash of the customer number from the joined customer master, linking to the Account table.', 'source_columns': ['kunden.kundennummer', 'opps.kunden_ref']}, {'explanation': 'Directly carries the original opportunity identifier from the source system for lineage and auditing.', 'target_column': 'Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c', 'source_columns': ['opps.opp_kennung']}, {'target_column': 'CreatedDate', 'explanation': 'Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.', 'source_columns': []}, {'source_columns': [], 'explanation': 'Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column.', 'target_column': 'LastModifiedDate'}, {'target_column': 'IsDeleted', 'explanation': 'Defaults to 0 as there is no source column to indicate deletion status.', 'source_columns': []}], 'target_table': 'Opportunity', 'dbt_sql': '
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(opps.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(opps.titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertangebot') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'identifizierung von entscheidungsträgern') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preiskalkulation') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung/überprüfung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen verloren') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting' -- Fallback as per plan
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD'),
        TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD'),
        TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD'),
        TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD'),
        TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD') -- Default to current date if unparseable or NULL
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN opps.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(opps.auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                CASE

                        REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,]+', '', 'g')), '.', ''), '''', '.')

    TRIM(UPPER(opps.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(kunden.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opps.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opps
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunden
    ON opps.kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.

- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 32, 42, 40327, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Project__c` failed.

```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(p.projekt_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(p.projektname), 'Unknown Project') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'active' THEN 'Active'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'on hold' THEN 'On Hold'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'cancelled' THEN 'Cancelled'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Project_Status__c",
    CASE
        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(CAST(p.go_live_datum AS DATE), 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Go_Live_Date__c",
    MD5(k.kundennummer) AS "Account__c",
    MD5(o.opp_kennung) AS "Opportunity__c",
    p.projekt_kennung AS "Legacy_Project_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_projekte') }} AS p
LEFT JOIN

LEFT JOIN

    ON p.opp_kennung_ref = o.opp_kennung

```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 33, 25, 861362, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Your previous dbt model for `Opportunity` failed.

Previous SQL:
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(opps.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(opps.titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertangebot') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'identifizierung von entscheidungsträgern') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preiskalkulation') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung/überprüfung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen verloren') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\/\d{2}\/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD') -- Default to current date if all parsing fails or NULL
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CASE
        WHEN opps.auftragswert IS NULL OR TRIM(opps.auftragswert) = '' THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CAST(
                CASE
                    WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.auftragswert) > 0 AND POSITION('.' IN opps.auftragswert) > 0 THEN -- Both dot and comma present, assume European
                        REPLACE(REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,]+', '', 'g')), '.', ''), ',', '.')
                    WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.auftragswert) > 0 THEN -- Only comma present, assume European decimal
                        REPLACE(TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9,]+', '', 'g')), ',', '.')
                    ELSE -- Only dot or no separator, assume US decimal
                        TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.]+', '', 'g'))
                END
            AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
    END AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opps.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(kunden.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opps.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opps
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunden
    ON opps.kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer
```

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH cleaned_opportunities AS (
    SELECT
        opps.opp_kennung,
        opps.titel,
        opps.vertriebsphase,
        opps.zieldatum,
        opps.waehrungscode,
        -- Pre-clean auftragswert to simplify subsequent parsing
        TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,]+', '', 'g')) AS cleaned_auftragswert,
        kunden.kundennummer
    FROM
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opps
    LEFT JOIN
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunden
        ON opps.kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer
)
SELECT
    MD5(opps.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(opps.titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertangebot') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'identifizierung von entscheidungsträgern') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preiskalkulation') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung/überprüfung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen verloren') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        NULLIF(
            CASE
                WHEN opps.cleaned_auftragswert IS NULL OR opps.cleaned_auftragswert = '' THEN NULL
                WHEN POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 AND POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) THEN
                            -- European format (e.g., 1.234,56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')
                        ELSE
                            -- US format (e.g., 1,234.56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '')
                    END
                WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    -- Only comma present, assume European decimal (e.g., 1234,56 -> 1234.56)
                    REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '.')
                ELSE
                    -- Only dot, or neither, assume US decimal (e.g., 1234.56 or 1234)
                    opps.cleaned_auftragswert
            END,
            ''
        ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opps.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(opps.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opps.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    cleaned_opportunities AS opps

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- opp_kennung: Generates a unique ID for the opportunity using an MD5 hash of the source opportunity ID.
  - Name <- titel: Uses the opportunity title, defaulting to 'Unknown Opportunity' if null or empty.
  - StageName <- vertriebsphase: Maps source sales phases to a standardized set of Salesforce opportunity stages, defaulting to 'Prospecting'.
  - CloseDate <- zieldatum: Parses the target date from various source formats (YYYY-MM-DD, DD.MM.YYYY, MM/DD/YYYY, YYYYMMDD) and formats it as 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Defaults to the current date if all parsing fails.
  - Amount <- auftragswert: Cleans and converts the `auftragswert` to a double precision number, handling both European and US decimal/thousand separators.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode: Converts the source currency code to uppercase and trims whitespace.
  - AccountId <- kunden_ref, kundennummer: Generates a Salesforce-style Account ID by hashing the customer number, derived from a join with the customers table.
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung: Populated directly from the source opportunity identifier for verification.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Set to 0, indicating the record is not deleted.
Reasoning: The previous error was likely due to complex nesting and repeated `REGEXP_REPLACE` calls within the `Amount` calculation, which can sometimes lead to parser issues. I introduced a Common Table Expression (CTE) to pre-clean the `auftragswert` column, simplifying the main `SELECT` statement and making the amount parsing logic more robust and readable. All other column transformations remain consistent with the requirements and best practices.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 35, 18, 875677, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH cleaned_opportunities AS (
    SELECT
        opps.opp_kennung,
        opps.titel,
        opps.vertriebsphase,
        opps.zieldatum,
        opps.waehrungscode,
        -- Pre-clean auftragswert to simplify subsequent parsing
        TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,]+', '', 'g')) AS cleaned_auftragswert,
        kunden.kundennummer
    FROM
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opps
    LEFT JOIN
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunden
        ON opps.kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer
)
SELECT
    MD5(opps.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(opps.titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertangebot') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'identifizierung von entscheidungsträgern') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preiskalkulation') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung/überprüfung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen verloren') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        NULLIF(
            CASE
                WHEN opps.cleaned_auftragswert IS NULL OR opps.cleaned_auftragswert = '' THEN NULL
                WHEN POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 AND POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) THEN
                            -- European format (e.g., 1.234,56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')
                        ELSE
                            -- US format (e.g., 1,234.56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '')
                    END
                WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    -- Only comma present, assume European decimal (e.g., 1234,56 -> 1234.56)
                    REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '.')
                ELSE
                    -- Only dot, or neither, assume US decimal (e.g., 1234.56 or 1234)
                    opps.cleaned_auftragswert
            END,
            ''
        ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opps.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(opps.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opps.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    cleaned_opportunities AS opps

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- opp_kennung: Generates a unique ID for the opportunity using an MD5 hash of the source opportunity ID.
  - Name <- titel: Uses the opportunity title, defaulting to 'Unknown Opportunity' if null or empty.
  - StageName <- vertriebsphase: Maps source sales phases to a standardized set of Salesforce opportunity stages, defaulting to 'Prospecting'.
  - CloseDate <- zieldatum: Parses the target date from various source formats (YYYY-MM-DD, DD.MM.YYYY, MM/DD/YYYY, YYYYMMDD) and formats it as 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Defaults to the current date if all parsing fails.
  - Amount <- auftragswert: Cleans and converts the `auftragswert` to a double precision number, handling both European and US decimal/thousand separators.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode: Converts the source currency code to uppercase and trims whitespace.
  - AccountId <- kunden_ref, kundennummer: Generates a Salesforce-style Account ID by hashing the customer number, derived from a join with the customers table.
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung: Populated directly from the source opportunity identifier for verification.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Set to 0, indicating the record is not deleted.
Reasoning: The previous error was likely due to complex nesting and repeated `REGEXP_REPLACE` calls within the `Amount` calculation, which can sometimes lead to parser issues. I introduced a Common Table Expression (CTE) to pre-clean the `auftragswert` column, simplifying the main `SELECT` statement and making the amount parsing logic more robust and readable. All other column transformations remain consistent with the requirements and best practices.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 36, 6, 496581, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

# Proposed mapping

## Account
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(master_kunden.kundennummer) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(master_kunden.unternehmensname), 'Unknown Account') AS "Name",
    master_kunden.erp_nr AS "ERP_Number__c",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(master_kunden.kundenklasse) = 'gold' THEN 'Gold'
        WHEN LOWER(master_kunden.kundenklasse) = 'silver' THEN 'Silver'
        WHEN LOWER(master_kunden.kundenklasse) = 'bronze' THEN 'Bronze'
        WHEN LOWER(master_kunden.kundenklasse) = 'platinum' THEN 'Platinum'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Customer_Tier__c",
    master_kunden.vertriebsgebiet AS "Region__c",
    master_kunden.industrie AS "Industry",
    master_kunden.homepage AS "Website",
    master_kunden.stadt AS "BillingCity",
    master_kunden.land_region AS "BillingCountry",
    master_kunden.kundennummer AS "Legacy_Customer_ID__c",
    NULL::TEXT AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::TEXT AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0::INTEGER AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS master_kunden
```
Column notes:
  - Id <- kundennummer: Generated a unique Salesforce-style ID using an MD5 hash of the source customer number.
  - Name <- unternehmensname: Mapped from the company name, defaulting to 'Unknown Account' if null or empty to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - ERP_Number__c <- erp_nr: Directly mapped from the source ERP number.
  - Customer_Tier__c <- kundenklasse: Mapped the source customer class to the target enum values (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum), with case-insensitive matching. Null if no match.
  - Region__c <- vertriebsgebiet: Directly mapped from the source sales region.
  - Industry <- industrie: Directly mapped from the source industry.
  - Website <- homepage: Directly mapped from the source homepage URL.
  - BillingCity <- stadt: Directly mapped from the source city.
  - BillingCountry <- land_region: Directly mapped from the source country/region.
  - Legacy_Customer_ID__c <- kundennummer: Stores the original customer number from the source system for auditing and cross-referencing.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column for creation date.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as there is no corresponding source column for last modification date.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Defaulted to 0 as there is no source column to indicate if the record is deleted.
Reasoning: A global mapping plan for all target tables from the `fixture_master_v2_src` database. Salesforce-style `Id` fields (e.g., `Id`, `AccountId`, `Account__c`, `Opportunity__c`, `Project__c`) will be generated consistently across tables using MD5 hashes of their respective source natural keys to ensure uniqueness and consistency. All `Legacy_*__c` columns will directly store the original natural key from the source system for auditing. Enum-typed target columns will involve case-insensitive mapping and normalization of source values to the defined enum domain, with `NULL` as the fallback for unmatched values. Dates will be parsed from varied source text formats into a consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` format, preferring `NULL` for unparseable or missing dates. Amounts will be cleaned of currency symbols and locale-specific separators before casting to `DOUBLE PRECISION`. Foreign key relationships will be resolved by joining on source natural keys and then mapping to the generated Salesforce `Id`s. `NOT NULL` target columns will always be populated, using meaningful default values if the source is empty. `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate` will default to `NULL` and `IsDeleted` to `0` in the absence of source data. Text normalization (`TRIM`, `INITCAP`, `LOWER`) will be applied as needed for data quality.
The Account model is derived from the master_kunden source table. A unique Id is generated using an MD5 hash of the kundennummer. Enum values for Customer_Tier__c are mapped with case-insensitivity, and other fields are directly mapped or defaulted according to the plan.

## Contact
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    MD5(mk.kontakt_id) AS "Id",
    TRIM(mk.rufname) AS "FirstName",
    COALESCE(TRIM(mk.familienname), 'Unknown Contact') AS "LastName",
    TRIM(mk.kontakt_email) AS "Email",
    TRIM(mk.tel) AS "Phone",
    TRIM(mk.berufsbezeichnung) AS "Title",
    CASE LOWER(TRIM(mk.rolle))
        WHEN 'decision maker' THEN 'Decision Maker'
        WHEN 'end user' THEN 'End User'
        WHEN 'technical contact' THEN 'Technical Contact'
        WHEN 'executive sponsor' THEN 'Executive Sponsor'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Role__c",
    CASE UPPER(TRIM(mk.korrespondenzsprache))
        WHEN 'DE' THEN 'DE'
        WHEN 'EN' THEN 'EN'
        WHEN 'FR' THEN 'FR'
        WHEN 'ES' THEN 'ES'
        WHEN 'IT' THEN 'IT'
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "Preferred_Language__c",
    MD5(mku.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    mk.kontakt_id AS "Legacy_Contact_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kontakte') }} AS mk
LEFT JOIN
    {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS mku
ON
    mk.kd_nummer = mku.kundennummer

```

  - Id <- master_kontakte.kontakt_id: Generated using an MD5 hash of the source contact ID for a unique and consistent Salesforce-style ID.
  - FirstName <- master_kontakte.rufname: Mapped from the source contact's first name, with leading/trailing whitespace removed.
  - LastName <- master_kontakte.familienname: Mapped from the source contact's last name, with a fallback to 'Unknown Contact' to satisfy the NOT NULL constraint.
  - Email <- master_kontakte.kontakt_email: Mapped from the source contact's email address, with leading/trailing whitespace removed.
  - Phone <- master_kontakte.tel: Mapped from the source contact's phone number, with leading/trailing whitespace removed.

## Opportunity
```sql

{{ config(materialized='table') }}

WITH cleaned_opportunities AS (
    SELECT
        opps.opp_kennung,
        opps.titel,
        opps.vertriebsphase,
        opps.zieldatum,
        opps.waehrungscode,
        -- Pre-clean auftragswert to simplify subsequent parsing
        TRIM(REGEXP_REPLACE(opps.auftragswert, '[^0-9.,]+', '', 'g')) AS cleaned_auftragswert,
        kunden.kundennummer
    FROM
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_opportunities') }} AS opps
    LEFT JOIN
        {{ source('fixture_master_v2_src', 'master_kunden') }} AS kunden
        ON opps.kunden_ref = kunden.kundennummer
)
SELECT
    MD5(opps.opp_kennung) AS "Id",
    COALESCE(TRIM(opps.titel), 'Unknown Opportunity') AS "Name",
    CASE
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('prospecting') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('qualification', 'qualifizierung') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('needs analysis', 'bedarfsanalyse') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('value proposition', 'wertangebot') THEN 'Value Proposition'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('id. decision makers', 'identifizierung von entscheidungsträgern') THEN 'Id. Decision Makers'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('perception analysis', 'wahrnehmungsanalyse') THEN 'Perception Analysis'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('proposal/price quote', 'angebot/preiskalkulation') THEN 'Proposal/Price Quote'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('negotiation/review', 'verhandlung/überprüfung') THEN 'Negotiation/Review'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed won', 'abgeschlossen gewonnen') THEN 'Closed Won'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(opps.vertriebsphase)) IN ('closed lost', 'abgeschlossen verloren') THEN 'Closed Lost'
        ELSE 'Prospecting'
    END AS "StageName",
    COALESCE(
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{2}/\d{2}/\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'MM/DD/YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        CASE WHEN opps.zieldatum ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(opps.zieldatum, 'YYYYMMDD'), 'YYYY-MM-DD') ELSE NULL END,
        TO_CHAR(CURRENT_DATE, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    ) AS "CloseDate",
    CAST(
        NULLIF(
            CASE
                WHEN opps.cleaned_auftragswert IS NULL OR opps.cleaned_auftragswert = '' THEN NULL
                WHEN POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 AND POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    CASE
                        WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > POSITION('.' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) THEN
                            -- European format (e.g., 1.234,56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, '.', ''), ',', '.')
                        ELSE
                            -- US format (e.g., 1,234.56 -> 1234.56)
                            REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '')
                    END
                WHEN POSITION(',' IN opps.cleaned_auftragswert) > 0 THEN
                    -- Only comma present, assume European decimal (e.g., 1234,56 -> 1234.56)
                    REPLACE(opps.cleaned_auftragswert, ',', '.')
                ELSE
                    -- Only dot, or neither, assume US decimal (e.g., 1234.56 or 1234)
                    opps.cleaned_auftragswert
            END,
            ''
        ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION
    ) AS "Amount",
    TRIM(UPPER(opps.waehrungscode)) AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    MD5(opps.kundennummer) AS "AccountId",
    opps.opp_kennung AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL::text AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL::text AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"
FROM
    cleaned_opportunities AS opps

```
Column notes:
  - Id <- opp_kennung: Generates a unique ID for the opportunity using an MD5 hash of the source opportunity ID.
  - Name <- titel: Uses the opportunity title, defaulting to 'Unknown Opportunity' if null or empty.
  - StageName <- vertriebsphase: Maps source sales phases to a standardized set of Salesforce opportunity stages, defaulting to 'Prospecting'.
  - CloseDate <- zieldatum: Parses the target date from various source formats (YYYY-MM-DD, DD.MM.YYYY, MM/DD/YYYY, YYYYMMDD) and formats it as 'YYYY-MM-DD'. Defaults to the current date if all parsing fails.
  - Amount <- auftragswert: Cleans and converts the `auftragswert` to a double precision number, handling both European and US decimal/thousand separators.
  - CurrencyIsoCode <- waehrungscode: Converts the source currency code to uppercase and trims whitespace.
  - AccountId <- kunden_ref, kundennummer: Generates a Salesforce-style Account ID by hashing the customer number, derived from a join with the customers table.
  - Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c <- opp_kennung: Populated directly from the source opportunity identifier for verification.
  - CreatedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - LastModifiedDate <- (none): Set to NULL as source data is not available.
  - IsDeleted <- (none): Set to 0, indicating the record is not deleted.
Reasoning: The previous error was likely due to complex nesting and repeated `REGEXP_REPLACE` calls within the `Amount` calculation, which can sometimes lead to parser issues. I introduced a Common Table Expression (CTE) to pre-clean the `auftragswert` column, simplifying the main `SELECT` statement and making the amount parsing logic more robust and readable. All other column transformations remain consistent with the requirements and best practices.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 6, 20, 37, 4, 882140, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='A reviewer rejected your dbt model for `Project__c`.

        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'completed' THEN 'Completed'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(p.projektstatus)) = 'in planning' THEN 'In Planning'

        WHEN p.go_live_datum ~ '^\d{2}\.\d{2}\.\d{4}$' THEN TO_CHAR(TO_DATE(p.go_live_datum, 'DD.MM.YYYY'), 'YYYY-MM-DD')

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
